title: Comparative: Emergency
lead: India: a ==constitutionally codified, centralised== emergency system (Part XVIII). US: ==statute-driven, decentralised== — emergency power scattered across laws like the National Emergencies Act.

head: Basis
India
United States
rows: Design
==Express code — Arts 352–360== (Part XVIII).
No consolidated code; only ==habeas corpus suspension== (rebellion/invasion) is express; rest via ==NEA, Stafford Act, IEEPA==.
Nature
==Constitutional emergency power== — temporary centralisation authorised by the text.
==Statutory-executive power== — President activates what Congress has granted.
Model
Centralised: Union acquires authority over ==States, rights, finances==.
Decentralised: declaration doesn't transform federalism or suspend rights.
Philosophy
Constitution ==anticipates breakdowns== — exceptional powers for national survival.
Constitution ==distrusts undefined executive power== — congressional authorisation required.
Primary actor
President — but ==Union Cabinet's written recommendation== required (Art 352, 44th CA).
President declares under statutes; ==Congress creates, limits, terminates==.
Legislative control
Parliament approves and ==periodically continues== proclamations (special majority for Art 352).
==National Emergencies Act==: congressional review; termination by joint resolution.
Rights impact
==Art 358== suspends Art 19 (war/external aggression); ==Art 359== suspends enforcement of specified FRs — ==never Arts 20 & 21==.
Rights ==not automatically suspended==; only habeas corpus, in rebellion/invasion.
Federal impact
Strong: unitary shift; ==President's Rule can replace state governments==; financial directions.
Limited: the federal government ==cannot dismiss state governments==.
Judicial review
Available — [[SR Bommai (1994)]]: Art 356 proclamations reviewable.
Strong — [[Youngstown]]: presidential emergency claims limited without constitutional/congressional authority.
